executiveSummary.text:
Kerry Group has been a deeply disappointing long-term investment — down ~25% over 1 year and
              ~26% over 5 years, and currently trading ~31% below its 52-week high of €100.50. The
              stock is sitting just above its 52-week low of €64.35. This is a business in transition:
              a high-quality global ingredients and taste solutions platform undergoing portfolio
              optimisation, with a new CEO at the helm, and showing early signs of EBITDA margin

businessModel.text:
Kerry Group is a global leader in taste and nutrition solutions — essentially a
              business-to-business ingredients company that supplies flavours, nutritional
              ingredients, and sustainable proteins to food and beverage manufacturers.
              Think of it as the invisible ingredient layer in most processed foods:
              the flavour in your yoghurt, the nutritional profile of your cereal, the
              protein hit in your sports drink.
            
            
              Revenue is split across two divisions:
            
            
              Taste & Nutrition (~70%): Flavours, sensory ingredients,
                nutritional ingredients, clean-label solutions. Serves big food companies
                (Nestle, Unilever, PepsiCo) who want to differentiate their products.
              Consumer Foods (~30%): Branded and private-label consumer
                food products, primarily in the UK and Ireland. This is the lower-margin,
                slower-growth business that Kerry has been gradually exiting.
            
            
              Economic moat: Kerry's moat is built on application expertise, long customer
              relationships (decades of co-development with major food companies), and its
              global manufacturing footprint. The clean-label and "free-from" trend is a
              genuine tailwind — replacing artificial ingredients with natural alternatives
              is complex and Kerry has scale to invest in this. However, the moat is not
              as wide as premium specialty chemical or pharmaceutical ingredient suppliers.

financialSnapshot.text:
Note: Full FY2025 financials were reported recently (March 2026). Key metrics confirmed: revenue growth in line with guidance, EBITDA margin expansion achieved. Precise figures from the LSE/ISE filings should be confirmed before trading.

  • Market cap: ~€11-12B (implied at €69.10 × ~165M shares)
  • Revenue (FY2025): ~€7.5-8B (confirm from filings)
  • EBITDA margin: FY2025 results showed expansion — positive signal that the portfolio optimisation and operational efficiency programme is working
  • Trailing PE: ~20-22x (estimated, based on current price and consensus earnings)
  • Dividend yield: ~2-3% (confirmed from LSE data)
  • Net debt / EBITDA: Moderate leverage, being reduced
  • Revenue growth (FY2025): Low-to-mid single digit, in line with guidance — not exciting but not deteriorating

Kerry Group has historically traded at a premium multiple (25-30x) because of its growth profile and market position. The compression to ~20-22x reflects the market's re-assessment of long-term growth prospects, particularly given the challenges in the consumer foods division and margin pressure across the ingredients sector.

thesisEvaluation.text:
Bull (25%)
                Consumer Foods exits complete. Emerging market
                revenue hits 30%+ of mix. Pipeline (clean-label, protein) drives volume re-acceleration.
                New CEO delivers on margin targets ahead of schedule. Stock recovers to €90-100.
                Upside: €90-100.
              
              
                Base (55%)
                Steady progress on margin expansion. Consumer
                Foods drag diminishes. Low single-digit revenue growth. Dividend maintained and
                possibly grown. Stock drifts to €75-85. Upside: €75-85.
              
              
                Bear (20%)
                Margin expansion stalls. Consumer Foods
                divestitures create one-time hits. Input cost inflation returns. Emerging market
                expansion disappoints. Stock falls to €50-60, in line with historical trough
                multiples. Downside: €50-60.
              
            

            
              Expected value: (25% × €92) + (55% × €80) + (20% × €55) = €23 + €44 + €11
              = ~€78 base case. Current price €69.10 is below the base case —
              the market is pricing in something close to the bear. That creates some
              theoretical upside from here, but the path is long and the timing uncertain.

risks.risk:
Consumer Foods drag (MEDIUM): The consumer foods division
                is a lower-margin, slower-growth business. Divesting it creates one-time
                disruption costs and leaves Kerry more concentrated in ingredients — which
                is not without its own challenges.

Customer concentration (MEDIUM): As a B2B supplier,
                Kerry's top customers are enormous food multinationals with significant
                bargaining power. Losing a major account or a key product launch would
                have an outsized impact.

Input cost volatility (MEDIUM): Commodity inputs
                (agricultural products, chemicals) are subject to price swings.
                Kerry can usually pass these on to customers, but with a lag —
                and big customers will push back.

Clean-label commoditisation (MEDIUM): The natural/
                clean-label trend is real, but as it becomes mainstream, more competitors
                can participate. Kerry needs to stay ahead through innovation and
                application expertise, not just "natural ingredients."

Emerging market execution (LOW–MEDIUM): Kerry's growth
                strategy relies heavily on expanding in Asia, Africa, and Latin America.
                These markets bring currency risk, regulatory complexity, and local
                competitors. The payoff is not guaranteed.

Valuation risk (LOW): At ~20-22x trailing earnings,
                Kerry is not obviously cheap — it is simply less expensive than it was.
                If earnings disappoint, the multiple compresses further.

recommendation.text:
HOLD. The stock is near the bottom of its range but
              that alone is not a buy signal. Kerry needs to demonstrate that the
              FY2025 margin expansion is the beginning of a trend, not a one-time
              event.

entryExit.text:
HOLD. The stock is near the bottom of its range but
              that alone is not a buy signal. Kerry needs to demonstrate that the
              FY2025 margin expansion is the beginning of a trend, not a one-time
              event.
            
            
              Key levels:
            
            
              €64–65 or below: Watch closely — this is the 52w low
                zone and a potential accumulation area if the fundamental story is
                intact. A confirmed break below €64 changes the picture negatively.
              €69–80: Hold and wait. No urgency to add, no reason
                to sell. The stock is in a recovery attempt but needs catalyst to
                break through €80 resistance.
              €80–85: First meaningful resistance. A sustained break
                above €80 would be the early signal of a re-rating. Trim or take
                profit if the fundamental story hasn't changed at that point.
              €90+: Overvalued relative to current growth profile —
                would require a meaningful upward earnings revision to justify
            
            
              Kerry Group is a classic "prove it" stock — the market wants to see
              the margin expansion and emerging market growth materialize before
              according it a higher multiple. The next quarterly update or
              investor day will be worth watching. Hold for now.
            
          

          
            Conviction Trend
            Latest conviction: 58/100. Trend versus prior report: Flat.
